Another novel by this author features a red heifer painted with white spots as part of a plan to blow up the Dome of the Rock. In that novel by this author, an inhaler on a cardboard chess set leads a detective to the killer of the messianic heroin addict Mendel Shpilman. The protagonist of a novel by this author flees the (*) Nazis in a crate containing the Prague Golem. An alternate history novel by this author is set in a Jewish-inhabited Sitka, Alaska. This author won a Pulitzer for a novel about two Jewish cousins who create Empire Comics and the World War II superhero The Escapist. For 10 points, name this author of The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier & Clay.
6a028dc3e7d76fea3790224d.answer: Michael Chabon (“SHAY-bon”) (The other novel is The Yiddish Policemen’s Union.)
6a028dc3e7d76fea3790224d.answer_sanitized: Michael Chabon ("SHAY-bon") (The other novel is The Yiddish Policemen's Union.)

6a028dc3e7d76fea379022ac.question: This thinker coined the term misarchism while describing the intrusion of democratic ideals into the sciences. Alfred Adler’s school of individual psychology was based on a concept from this thinker that he characterized as striving for superiority. This thinker compared truth to metaphor and illusion in On Truth and Lies in a Nonmoral Sense, and called for a “transvaluation of values” in The (*) Antichrist. This thinker reimagined the Stoic idea of “eternal recurrence” and praises Richard Wagner in a work contrasting the “Apollonian” and “Dionysian.” This thinker contrasted master and slave morality and analyzed the title art form in The Birth of Tragedy. For 10 points, name this German author of Beyond Good and Evil, who coined the statement “God is dead.”
6a028dc3e7d76fea379022ac.question_sanitized: This thinker coined the term misarchism while describing the intrusion of democratic ideals into the sciences. Alfred Adler's school of individual psychology was based on a concept from this thinker that he characterized as striving for superiority. This thinker compared truth to metaphor and illusion in On Truth and Lies in a Nonmoral Sense, and called for a "transvaluation of values" in The (*) Antichrist. This thinker reimagined the Stoic idea of "eternal recurrence" and praises Richard Wagner in a work contrasting the "Apollonian" and "Dionysian." This thinker contrasted master and slave morality and analyzed the title art form in The Birth of Tragedy. For 10 points, name this German author of Beyond Good and Evil, who coined the statement "God is dead."
6a028dc3e7d76fea379022ac.answer: Friedrich Nietzsche [or Friedrich Wilhelm Nietzsche] (The first line refers to The Antichrist, and the second to Adler’s formulation of the “will to power.”)
6a028dc3e7d76fea379022ac.answer_sanitized: Friedrich Nietzsche [or Friedrich Wilhelm Nietzsche] (The first line refers to The Antichrist, and the second to Adler's formulation of the "will to power.")

6a028dc3e7d76fea379022b7.leadin_sanitized: One leader of this institution gave a speech praising justice, only to refute that speech the very next day. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this institution led by Carneades, which expanded on ideas like the immortality of the soul and the world of forms created by its founder, Plato.

Carneades and his predecessor Arcesilaus led the Academy during a period when it was associated with this school of thought. Sextus Empiricus wrote a book titled “Outlines of” one figure in this school of thought.

The leaders of the Skeptical Academy disagreed with this thinker’s doctrine of Katalepsis, which he demonstrated by closing his fist. This student of Crates proposed a threefold philosophy of physics, logic, and ethics.

6a028dc4e7d76fea379022cc.question: An Austrian art historian drew on this thinker’s ideas in Art and Illusion to develop the notions of “making and matching” and “trial and error” in art. In a work dedicated to longtime friend Friedrich Hayek, this thinker described a formula that combines truth content and falsity content to evaluate an assertion’s “truthlikeness,” which he called verisimilitude. This author of (*) Conjectures and Refutations decried the “Spell of Plato” in the first section of a work that describes his paradox of tolerance. This thinker tried to solve the “demarcation problem” in The Logic of Scientific Discovery, which also introduced his idea that hypotheses must be falsifiable. For 10 points, name this Austrian-born philosopher of science who wrote The Open Society and Its Enemies.
